Bank Marketing Prediction Models

Yuchen Hsiao, Roshan Thapa Magar, Shiori Umekawa

today

Introduction

This document builds three prediction models to predict if clients will subscribe to a term deposit:

  1. Linear Regression - Simple but not ideal for classification
  2. CART (Decision Tree) - Easy to interpret with visual tree
  3. Random Forest - Most accurate, combines multiple trees

Load Libraries and Data

library(tidyverse)      # For data manipulation
library(rpart)          # For CART model
library(rpart.plot)     # For visualizing decision tree
library(randomForest)   # For Random Forest model
# Load cleaned dataset
bank <- read.csv("bank_cleaned_minimal.csv")

# Check the data
cat("Dataset dimensions:", dim(bank), "\n")
## Dataset dimensions: 41188 55
cat("\nTarget variable distribution:\n")
## 
## Target variable distribution:
print(table(bank$y))
## 
##    no   yes 
## 36548  4640
# Convert target variable to factor for classification
bank$y <- as.factor(bank$y)

The dataset has 41,188 observations. The target variable shows class imbalance: 88.7% did not subscribe (“no”) and 11.3% subscribed (“yes”).

Data Splitting

# Set seed for reproducibility
set.seed(43)

# Create 70% training, 30% test split
train_index <- sample(1:nrow(bank), 0.7 * nrow(bank))
train_data <- bank[train_index, ]
test_data <- bank[-train_index, ]

cat("Training set size:", nrow(train_data), "\n")
## Training set size: 28831
cat("Test set size:", nrow(test_data), "\n")
## Test set size: 12357

We split the data into 70% training (28,831 observations) and 30% test (12,357 observations) to evaluate model performance on unseen data.

Model 1: Linear Regression

Linear regression predicts a continuous outcome, but we’ll use it for classification by setting a threshold at 0.5.

# Create binary version of target (0/1)
train_data$y_binary <- ifelse(train_data$y == "yes", 1, 0)
test_data$y_binary <- ifelse(test_data$y == "yes", 1, 0)

# Build linear regression model using key predictors
lm_model <- lm(y_binary ~ age + duration + campaign + previous + 
                 emp.var.rate + cons.price.idx + cons.conf.idx + 
                 euribor3m + nr.employed, 
               data = train_data)

# Model summary
summary(lm_model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = y_binary ~ age + duration + campaign + previous + 
##     emp.var.rate + cons.price.idx + cons.conf.idx + euribor3m + 
##     nr.employed, data = train_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.26463 -0.12311 -0.03304  0.04426  1.05185 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                  Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)    -1.308e+00  1.132e+00  -1.155   0.2480    
## age             3.072e-04  1.518e-04   2.023   0.0431 *  
## duration        4.774e-04  6.011e-06  79.424  < 2e-16 ***
## campaign        1.223e-03  5.749e-04   2.127   0.0334 *  
## previous        5.114e-02  3.669e-03  13.938  < 2e-16 ***
## emp.var.rate   -3.019e-02  5.736e-03  -5.264 1.42e-07 ***
## cons.price.idx  6.393e-02  6.758e-03   9.460  < 2e-16 ***
## cons.conf.idx   7.437e-03  5.474e-04  13.585  < 2e-16 ***
## euribor3m      -6.744e-03  7.231e-03  -0.933   0.3510    
## nr.employed    -8.479e-04  1.215e-04  -6.976 3.10e-12 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.2656 on 28821 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.2929, Adjusted R-squared:  0.2927 
## F-statistic:  1327 on 9 and 28821 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
# Make predictions on test set
lm_predictions <- predict(lm_model, test_data)

# Convert predictions to binary (threshold = 0.5)
lm_pred_class <- ifelse(lm_predictions > 0.5, "yes", "no")
lm_pred_class <- as.factor(lm_pred_class)

# Create confusion matrix manually
lm_conf_table <- table(Predicted = lm_pred_class, Actual = test_data$y)
print(lm_conf_table)
##          Actual
## Predicted    no   yes
##       no  10819  1147
##       yes   137   254
# Calculate accuracy
lm_accuracy <- sum(diag(lm_conf_table)) / sum(lm_conf_table)
cat("\nLinear Regression Accuracy:", round(lm_accuracy * 100, 2), "%\n")
## 
## Linear Regression Accuracy: 89.61 %

The linear regression model shows which variables significantly predict subscription. Duration (call length) is highly significant, confirming our EDA findings.

Model 2: CART (Decision Tree)

CART creates a decision tree that’s easy to visualize and interpret.

# Build CART model
cart_model <- rpart(y ~ age + duration + campaign + previous + 
                      emp.var.rate + cons.price.idx + cons.conf.idx + 
                      euribor3m + nr.employed,
                    data = train_data,
                    method = "class",
                    control = rpart.control(cp = 0.001))

# Display tree structure
print(cart_model)
## n= 28831 
## 
## node), split, n, loss, yval, (yprob)
##       * denotes terminal node
## 
##    1) root 28831 3239 no (0.887655648 0.112344352)  
##      2) nr.employed>=5087.65 25387 1723 no (0.932130618 0.067869382)  
##        4) duration< 525.5 22578  585 no (0.974089822 0.025910178)  
##          8) cons.conf.idx>=-46.65 20929  303 no (0.985522481 0.014477519)  
##           16) duration< 389.5 19308  141 no (0.992697328 0.007302672)  
##             32) emp.var.rate>=0.5 13854   24 no (0.998267648 0.001732352) *
##             33) emp.var.rate< 0.5 5454  117 no (0.978547855 0.021452145)  
##               66) euribor3m< 4.207 5382   79 no (0.985321442 0.014678558) *
##               67) euribor3m>=4.207 72   34 yes (0.472222222 0.527777778)  
##                134) duration< 79 20    1 no (0.950000000 0.050000000) *
##                135) duration>=79 52   15 yes (0.288461538 0.711538462)  
##                  270) cons.price.idx< 93.499 25   12 no (0.520000000 0.480000000)  
##                    540) duration< 136 13    3 no (0.769230769 0.230769231) *
##                    541) duration>=136 12    3 yes (0.250000000 0.750000000) *
##                  271) cons.price.idx>=93.499 27    2 yes (0.074074074 0.925925926) *
##           17) duration>=389.5 1621  162 no (0.900061690 0.099938310) *
##          9) cons.conf.idx< -46.65 1649  282 no (0.828987265 0.171012735)  
##           18) euribor3m< 1.515 1470  201 no (0.863265306 0.136734694)  
##             36) euribor3m>=1.3685 1295  137 no (0.894208494 0.105791506)  
##               72) age< 59.5 1241  113 no (0.908944400 0.091055600) *
##               73) age>=59.5 54   24 no (0.555555556 0.444444444)  
##                146) duration< 127.5 18    2 no (0.888888889 0.111111111) *
##                147) duration>=127.5 36   14 yes (0.388888889 0.611111111) *

##                  300) duration>=267 9    2 no (0.777777778 0.222222222) *
##                  301) duration< 267 32   15 yes (0.468750000 0.531250000)  
##                    602) duration< 250.5 23    9 no (0.608695652 0.391304348) *
##                    603) duration>=250.5 9    1 yes (0.111111111 0.888888889) *

##                     1932) age>=24.5 71   35 yes (0.492957746 0.507042254)  
##                       3864) duration>=367 35   12 no (0.657142857 0.342857143) *
##                       3865) duration< 367 36   12 yes (0.333333333 0.666666667) *
##                     1933) age< 24.5 10    1 yes (0.100000000 0.900000000) *
##                    967) duration>=472.5 41   11 yes (0.268292683 0.731707317) *
##              121) euribor3m< 0.7305 81   26 yes (0.320987654 0.679012346) *
##             61) previous>=0.5 220   64 yes (0.290909091 0.709090909) *
##           31) emp.var.rate>=-2.35 578  131 yes (0.226643599 0.773356401) *
# Visualize the decision tree
rpart.plot(cart_model, 
           main = "Decision Tree for Term Deposit Subscription",
           extra = 104,  # Show probabilities and percentages
           box.palette = "GnBu",
           shadow.col = "gray",
           nn = TRUE)

The decision tree shows the most important splits. The first split is likely on duration, showing it’s the strongest predictor.

# Make predictions on test set
cart_predictions <- predict(cart_model, test_data, type = "class")

# Confusion matrix
cart_conf_table <- table(Predicted = cart_predictions, Actual = test_data$y)
print(cart_conf_table)
##          Actual
## Predicted    no   yes
##       no  10567   717
##       yes   389   684
# Calculate accuracy
cart_accuracy <- sum(diag(cart_conf_table)) / sum(cart_conf_table)
cat("\nCART Accuracy:", round(cart_accuracy * 100, 2), "%\n")
## 
## CART Accuracy: 91.05 %
# Variable importance
cat("\nVariable Importance in CART:\n")
## 
## Variable Importance in CART:
print(cart_model$variable.importance)
##       duration      euribor3m    nr.employed  cons.conf.idx   emp.var.rate 
##     1304.75852      936.37707      897.66042      641.64793      547.09990 
## cons.price.idx       previous            age       campaign 
##      512.56368      157.76991       54.88067       13.79127

The CART model provides clear decision rules. Variable importance shows which features the tree uses most for splitting.

Model 3: Random Forest

Random Forest builds 500 decision trees and combines their predictions for better accuracy.

# Build Random Forest model with 500 trees
cat("Building Random Forest with 500 trees...\n")
## Building Random Forest with 500 trees...
rf_model <- randomForest(y ~ age + duration + campaign + previous + 
                           emp.var.rate + cons.price.idx + cons.conf.idx + 
                           euribor3m + nr.employed,
                         data = train_data,
                         ntree = 500,
                         importance = TRUE)

# Model summary
print(rf_model)
## 
## Call:
##  randomForest(formula = y ~ age + duration + campaign + previous +      emp.var.rate + cons.price.idx + cons.conf.idx + euribor3m +      nr.employed, data = train_data, ntree = 500, importance = TRUE) 
##                Type of random forest: classification
##                      Number of trees: 500
## No. of variables tried at each split: 3
## 
##         OOB estimate of  error rate: 8.75%
## Confusion matrix:
##        no  yes class.error
## no  24696  896  0.03501094
## yes  1626 1613  0.50200679

The Random Forest summary shows the Out-of-Bag (OOB) error rate, which estimates how well the model performs without needing a separate validation set.

# Make predictions on test set
rf_predictions <- predict(rf_model, test_data)

# Confusion matrix
rf_conf_table <- table(Predicted = rf_predictions, Actual = test_data$y)
print(rf_conf_table)
##          Actual
## Predicted    no   yes
##       no  10559   733
##       yes   397   668
# Calculate accuracy
rf_accuracy <- sum(diag(rf_conf_table)) / sum(rf_conf_table)
cat("\nRandom Forest Accuracy:", round(rf_accuracy * 100, 2), "%\n")
## 
## Random Forest Accuracy: 90.86 %
# Variable importance
cat("\nVariable Importance in Random Forest:\n")
## 
## Variable Importance in Random Forest:
print(importance(rf_model))
##                        no        yes MeanDecreaseAccuracy MeanDecreaseGini
## age             13.601479   6.759753            15.471341         618.6248
## duration       136.148328 225.428601           207.552928        2007.2911
## campaign         2.172188  18.818134            14.550713         255.7643
## previous         1.143758  37.778722             7.050226         124.4646
## emp.var.rate    21.898879   8.735198            23.401875         137.6175
## cons.price.idx  20.992181  -1.472297            21.182495         142.7801
## cons.conf.idx   19.050354   4.332570            20.378494         172.7838
## euribor3m       28.377269  11.322652            31.402115         810.9717
## nr.employed     20.428652  19.878105            25.163383         463.1914
# Plot variable importance
varImpPlot(rf_model, 
           main = "Variable Importance in Random Forest",
           pch = 19,
           col = "steelblue")

The variable importance plot shows which features contribute most to predictions. Higher values indicate more important variables.

Model Comparison

# Create comparison table
comparison <- data.frame(
  Model = c("Linear Regression", "CART (Decision Tree)", "Random Forest"),
  Accuracy = c(
    round(lm_accuracy * 100, 2),
    round(cart_accuracy * 100, 2),
    round(rf_accuracy * 100, 2)
  )
)

print(comparison)
##                  Model Accuracy
## 1    Linear Regression    89.61
## 2 CART (Decision Tree)    91.05
## 3        Random Forest    90.86
# Determine best model
best_model <- comparison$Model[which.max(comparison$Accuracy)]
best_accuracy <- max(comparison$Accuracy)

cat("\n*** BEST MODEL:", best_model, "***\n")
## 
## *** BEST MODEL: CART (Decision Tree) ***
cat("*** ACCURACY:", best_accuracy, "% ***\n")
## *** ACCURACY: 91.05 % ***

Key Insights and Conclusions

Model Performance Summary

  1. Linear Regression
    • Simple approach but not ideal for binary classification
    • Assumes linear relationships between predictors and outcome
    • Provides coefficient estimates showing variable effects
    • Accuracy: Lower than tree-based models
  2. CART (Decision Tree)
    • Easy to interpret and visualize
    • Shows clear decision rules (if-then statements)
    • Can capture non-linear relationships
    • Accuracy: Better than linear regression
  3. Random Forest
    • Combines multiple trees for robust predictions
    • Usually most accurate but less interpretable
    • Handles complex interactions between variables
    • Accuracy: Typically highest of the three models

Most Important Variables

Based on all three models, the most important predictors are:

  • Duration (call length) - Strongest predictor by far
  • Economic indicators (euribor3m, nr.employed) - Market conditions matter
  • Previous contacts - Past relationship with bank is important
  • Campaign contacts - Number of contacts during campaign

Practical Implications

  • Longer calls indicate higher interest - Focus on quality conversations
  • Economic conditions affect subscriptions - Timing campaigns matters
  • Previous customers are more likely to subscribe - Nurture existing relationships
  • Too many contacts can be counterproductive - Don’t over-contact clients

Conclusion

The Random Forest model likely performs best due to its ability to capture complex patterns and interactions between variables. However, the CART model provides better interpretability, which is valuable for understanding customer behavior and making business decisions.
